title: Sous le capot — the portal documents itself, publicly
problem: Most consulting portfolios show the polished result and hide the construction. That asymmetry feels dishonest to a buyer who's been burned before — they want to know HOW the work gets done, not just what shipped. /meta inverts the default: every feature documented in the codebase is rendered as a public card. Click one to expand the full record — problem, implementation, decisions (with rationales), success criteria, known limitations, status history. The same document the dev reads when modifying the code is the document the visitor reads to evaluate the dev. Build-in-public, structurally: no separate marketing artifact, no curated 'about how we work' page — just the feature.jsons that already drive the code.
analysis: Reads src/data/lac-features.json — the manifest written at prebuild by scripts/build-lac-meta.mjs from every *.feature.json co-located in the codebase. Each feature renders as a card with the title + status pill + tags; clicking expands it in place to show the full record. Status history is rendered chronologically (draft → active → frozen, with reasons + dates). Decisions display with their rationale + recommendation, not just the headline — that's what makes the page useful to a reader trying to evaluate the practice. The Scorecard component up top tallies coverage: how many features are frozen vs. active vs. draft, mean staleness in days, total decisions captured. A 'voir en direct' link on each card jumps to the documented surface so the visitor can read the doc and the artifact side by side. The page is static after prebuild — no runtime API call, the manifest is fixed at deploy time.
implementation: src/pages/Meta.tsx — reads ../data/lac-features.json as a static import (Vite bundles it). Renders a grid of feature cards with a single open-card state (useState<string | null>). Card body sections (analysis, decisions, success criteria, known limitations, status history) are conditionally rendered based on which fields the source feature.json filled. Date formatting via src/lib/format.ts. The Scorecard at the top reads the manifest once and computes aggregate stats inline. PageMast + FeatureContinue close the meta loop. lac-features.json is regenerated by scripts/build-lac-meta.mjs at prebuild — that script globs src/**/*.feature.json + functions/**/*.feature.json + the root feature.json, validates each against the schema, and writes the manifest. A feature with a missing required field falls into a 'flagged' bucket the script logs but doesn't fail on; Meta.tsx renders flagged features with a visual marker so it's visible to the public reader.

decisions.decision: The page renders directly from the same feature.json files the code uses — no separate marketing copy
decisions.rationale: If /meta read from a hand-curated 'public-facing version' of the docs, the public version would silently drift from reality the first time someone changed a feature.json without remembering to update the mirror. By rendering the same files, drift is structurally impossible — there's one source of truth.
decisions.recommendation: If a particular field needs to read differently for a public audience (e.g. trim the implementation prose), edit the feature.json itself. The internal LAC reader should be reading the public-style copy too — that's the point.

decisions.decision: Manifest is static at prebuild, not fetched at runtime
decisions.rationale: Loi 25 + boring-tech: no runtime fetch means no server log of who reads the meta page, no 503 path, no API to maintain. The manifest is also tiny (~50KB) so bundling it has zero performance cost. A future ten-fold growth in feature count would still be sub-megabyte.
decisions.recommendation: If the manifest ever crosses ~500KB, code-split per-feature: keep the index static, lazy-load individual feature docs. Don't introduce a runtime API.

decisions.decision: Show the full decision record — rationale and recommendation, not just headlines
decisions.rationale: A bulleted list of 'we decided X' reads like a marketing artifact and tells the reader nothing about why. A list with 'we decided X because Y, recommendation Z' reads like an engineering record and lets a buyer evaluate the judgment, not just the outcome. The cost is page length; the payoff is signal.
decisions.recommendation: When authoring a feature.json's decisions, write the rationale to a stranger. Don't shorthand internal context — assume the /meta reader is reading this as their first encounter with the project.

successCriteria:
- Every feature.json in the codebase appears on /meta at next deploy, with no additional manual step
- Status pills, status history, decisions, success criteria, and known limitations are all surfaced verbatim from the source files
- The Scorecard reflects current coverage stats at every deploy
- The 'voir en direct' link on each card resolves to the documented surface, working in both FR and EN
- The page makes zero runtime fetches
- A reader using /meta to evaluate the practice can see the same record an internal contributor reads
userGuide: Open /meta (or /en/meta) to see every documented feature of the portal. Each card shows title, status, and tags; click to expand and read the full record (problem, how it's built, decisions made with rationale, success criteria, known limitations, status history). The Scorecard at the top reflects coverage at this deploy. Each expanded card carries a 'voir en direct' link to the live surface. To add a feature to this page, drop a *.feature.json next to its code — build-lac-meta.mjs picks it up at the next build. To re-author a feature already documented, edit its co-located feature.json. The internal LAC tooling and this public page read the same file.
knownLimitations: lac-features.json is regenerated at prebuild — a feature.json edited but not committed before deploy won't appear until the next build runs

Schema validation in build-lac-meta.mjs is lenient (warn but don't fail) — a malformed feature.json shows up here with a 'flagged' marker rather than blocking the build; the rationale is that one bad feature.json shouldn't break the deploy
Decision records grow over time and never auto-collapse — a feature with 12 decisions reads as a long expanded card; readers are expected to scroll or skim by tags

/meta itself does not have a Scorecard entry for 'how many features.json are missing' — FEATURE-DOCS.md is the companion audit; the two are intentionally separate (public-facing showcase vs. internal gap tracker)

statusHistory.reason: Page shipped reading the LAC manifest; cards expand in place with full decision records.
Code-first doc co-located. Public showcase of the lifecycle continues to mirror the internal LAC reader's view 1:1.

revisions.author: marc+claude
revisions.reason: Initial author — P1 sweep, co-located doc for /meta.
